title: Hello World Notebook!
text:
This is an example of the Jupyter .ipynb document format
# This is an executable cell
print("Hello World!")

Hello World!
# Setting variables in Python
x = 42
x

42
# Variables persist after being set in previously executed cells
x

42

title: Hello World Notebook!
text:
Markdown Cells Support Rich Formatting
You can use: - Bold and italic text - Lists (like this one!) - Links - inline code - And even LaTeX math: \(E = mc^2\)
This makes notebooks great for explaining your code!

# You can perform calculations across cells
y = 10
z = x + y
print(f"x ({x}) + y ({y}) = {z}")


# Notebooks make it easy to import and use libraries
import math
import random

# Generate a random number and calculate its square root
num = random.randint(1, 100)
sqrt_num = math.sqrt(num)
print(f"The square root of {num} is {sqrt_num:.2f}")

The square root of 29 is 5.39



# Visualizations appear inline!
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x_values = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y_values = np.sin(x_values)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.plot(x_values, y_values)
plt.title('Sine Wave')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('sin(x)')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

title: Hello World Notebook!
section: What Happens When There’s an Error?
text:
What Happens When There’s an Error?
Run the cell below to see how notebooks handle errors.
The error appears in the output, but other cells continue to work.

# This will cause an error
result = 10 / 0


---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ZeroDivisionError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[9], line 2
      1 # This will cause an error
----> 2 result = 10 / 0

ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

title: Day 2: Working With Data
text:
What is a Notebook?

An interactive document that combines:

Code that can be executed
Rich text explanations (markdown)
Visualizations and outputs

Think of it as a computational story

Tell a story with code, data, and explanations

Originally designed for data science and research
Now, a fundamental tool for working with AI

title: Day 2: Working With Data
text:
A Brief History of Notebooks

2011: IPython Notebook project begins

Interactive Python shell → web-based notebook

2014: Renamed to Jupyter (Julia, Python, R)

Now supports 40+ programming languages
Python is most popular by far

2017: Google launches Colab

Free cloud-based Jupyter notebooks
Free access to GPUs and TPUs

Today: Industry standard for ML/AI development

title: Day 2: Working With Data
text:
Anatomy of a Python Notebook

Cells: Building blocks of notebooks

Code cells: Executable Python code
Markdown cells: Text, headings, images, equations

Outputs: Results appear directly below code cells

Text, tables, plots, interactive widgets

Kernel: The computational engine running your code

Maintains state between cell executions

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Days
Module
Focus




Week 1, Day 1
Intro
AI concepts survey, types of AI, setting the stage


Week 1, Days 2–3
Module 1: Working with Data
pandas, matplotlib, scikit-learn, Mongolian datasets


Week 1, Days 4–5
Module 2: Physical Computing
micro:bit V2, CreateAI, gesture ML models, MakeCode


Week 2, Day 6
Module 3: Computer Vision
CNNs, TF.js, MediaPipe, CV mini-projects


Week 2, Day 7
CV → GenAI Bridge
VLMs, tokens, embeddings, transformer overview


Week 2, Days 8–9
Module 4: Generative AI
Prompt engineering, chatbots, diffusion, image gen


Week 2, Day 10
Capstone Showcase
Final project presentations, reflection, celebration












Articulate what AI is and name 3+ real-world examples they interact with daily
Distinguish between supervised ML, computer vision, and generative AI
Preview all four modules and understand how the two weeks connect
Feel comfortable and curious — this is a safe space to experiment and be wrong













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Warm-Up (15 min)
“AI Around You” brainstorm: pairs list every AI they encountered in the last 24 hours. Share on whiteboard/Jamboard.
0:00–0:15
Icebreaker energy


Direct Instruction (20 min)
Instructor overview: types of AI (supervised ML, CV, generative AI). Teaser for each module with short visual clips — Naadam footage, an image classifier, a chatbot in action.
0:15–0:35
Keep slides visual


“Is It AI?” Activity (20 min)
Groups sort 20 tech examples (cards) into: AI / Not AI / Not Sure. Debrief as class.
0:35–0:55
Physical or Jamboard cards


Mini-Project (35 min)
“My AI Map”: students sketch a personal diagram of AI they use, categorized by type. Add one AI they wish existed.
0:55–1:30
Paper or slides


Share & Preview (20 min)
3–4 volunteers share maps. Instructor reveals the 10-day journey — the “syllabus reveal.” Preview tomorrow’s data deep-dive.
1:30–1:50



Exit Ticket (10 min)
One thing I knew · one thing I learned · one question I have. Collect for planning.
1:50–2:00








Miro
“Is it AI?” card set (printable or digital)
Short video clips from Code.org (~2 min)












Open a Colab notebook, run cells, and load a CSV with pandas
Use .head(), .describe(), .value_counts(), and filtering to investigate a real question
Produce at least one meaningful matplotlib visualization that supports a finding
Explain why data quality and selection matter for AI — using today’s dataset as evidence













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




The Hook: A Mystery (10 min)
Instructor opens with a single striking fact: in January, Ulaanbaatar regularly records PM2.5 levels 30–40× the WHO safe limit — worse than Beijing, worse than Delhi. Show a before/after satellite photo of the city in summer vs. winter smog. ‘Your job today: figure out why, using data.’ Introduce the detective framing: the dataset is your crime scene.
0:00–0:10
Show satellite comparison image


Setup & Tools (15 min)
Walk through Google Colab. Every student runs: print('Investigation begins!'). Quick pandas demo: DataFrames, .head(), .info(), .describe() — framed as ‘reading the crime scene.’
0:10–0:25
Pair struggling students


Investigation Sprint (40 min)
Groups of 3 dig into the Ulaanbaatar AQI dataset with guided question cards (not open-ended — each card is a specific clue to chase). E.g., ‘Which month has the worst PM2.5? Which has the best?’, ‘Is there a pattern by day of week?’, ‘Does temperature predict pollution level?’ Groups record findings in a shared doc.
0:25–1:05
Question cards pre-prepared


Visualize Your Evidence (25 min)
Each group turns their strongest finding into one chart using matplotlib templates. Frame: ‘A detective doesn’t just tell the jury — they show the evidence.’
1:05–1:30
Templates in notebook


AI Training Data Lens (20 min)
Pivot: ‘Now imagine you’re an AI company and this is your training data for a pollution prediction model. Would it be good enough? What’s missing? What would your model get wrong?’ Small group discussion, then share out.
1:30–1:50
Connects directly to Week 2


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Each group posts their one-sentence finding to a class board. Preview: tomorrow you get an AI assistant to help with a harder dataset — and you’ll decide if it’s trustworthy.
1:50–2:00








Google Colab (browser, no install)
Ulaanbaatar historical AQI dataset (PM2.5, PM10, temperature, season — CSV, pre-cleaned from aqicn.org or IQAir historical download)
Starter notebook with scaffolded cells + matplotlib templates
Investigation question cards (printed, one set per group — 6–8 clues to chase)
Shared class findings doc (Google Doc or Jamboard)
Satellite comparison images: Ulaanbaatar summer vs. winter smog (freely available)












Use a Claude-powered data assistant to query a dataset in plain English
Critically evaluate AI-generated interpretations against actual data
Train a simple linear regression model and interpret its predictions
Articulate the direct conceptual link between regression modeling and the micro:bit ML training in Module 2













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




The Hook: Dzud (10 min)
Instructor introduces the dzud — Mongolia’s winter livestock disaster. Show the stat: in 2010, 20 million animals died in a single winter. In 2024, 7.4 million in just four months. ‘Herders knew a bad summer meant a bad winter. Can a machine learn that relationship from data?’ Introduce today’s dataset: aimag-level livestock mortality, summer drought index, and winter temperature, 1990–2013.
0:00–0:10
Map of Mongolia’s 21 aimags on screen


Introduce the AI Co-Pilot (15 min)
Instructor demo: a Claude-powered chat interface loaded with the dzud dataset context. Ask it: ‘Which aimag suffered the most in 2010?’ Then ask something it gets wrong or oversimplifies. ‘Your job: use this tool as a starting point, then verify everything in code.’
0:10–0:25
Claude.ai Project or Colab widget


Investigation: Human + AI (35 min)
Groups explore the dataset with the AI co-pilot. Workflow: ask the assistant a question → verify in pandas → document where it’s right, wrong, or uncertain. Each group must catch the AI making at least 2 errors or oversimplifications before they present.
0:25–1:00
Encourage skepticism explicitly


Linear Regression: Predict the Disaster (25 min)
Instructor-led walkthrough: train a linear regression model to predict livestock mortality from summer drought index and winter temperature. Students run it step-by-step. Key question: ‘This model sees the summer data — can it warn a herder before winter arrives?’
1:00–1:25
Heavily scaffolded notebook


The Bridge Moment (5 min)
Instructor draws the explicit connection: ‘Yesterday we looked for patterns in pollution data. Today we trained a model to predict disasters from patterns in climate data. On Thursday, you’ll do the exact same thing — but the data will be your own body movement, and the micro:bit will be the device that learns.’ Plant the seed.
1:25–1:30
Key conceptual moment — don’t rush


Data Story Presentations (20 min)
Each group: 3 min presentation — the question, the evidence, one thing the AI got wrong, one limitation of the dataset.
1:30–1:50
Rubric: story, evidence, AI critique, limitation


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Class vote: whose finding was most surprising? ‘What does it feel like to catch an AI being wrong?’ Preview: micro:bits arrive tomorrow.
1:50–2:00








Claude-powered data assistant (Claude.ai Project with dataset summary as context, or a lightweight Colab widget using the Anthropic API)
Dzud dataset: aimag-level livestock mortality + summer drought index + winter temperature, 1990–2013 (sourced from Mongolian NSO / Rao et al. 2015 supplementary data)
Colab notebook with linear regression walkthrough + visualization templates
Map of Mongolia’s 21 aimags (printed or on-screen)
Presentation rubric (story, evidence, AI critique, limitation — 1 page)
Optional: class leaderboard for ‘best AI catch of the day’












Connect a micro:bit to CreateAI and view live accelerometer data in real time
Train a gesture model with at least 3 classes and explain what ‘confidence threshold’ means
Understand that ML models learn from examples, not explicit rules
Write a MakeCode program that responds meaningfully to detected gestures













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Hardware Orientation (15 min)
Distribute micro:bits (groups of 2–3). Connect via USB, open createai.microbit.org. Instructor demo: accelerometer graph while shaking vs. still.
0:00–0:15
V2 required for on-device ML


First Model: Walk vs. Still (25 min)
Guided: everyone trains the same model — ‘walking’ vs. ‘still’ vs. ‘other’. Collect 20 samples per class. Train and test on each other.
0:15–0:40
Use CreateAI guided tour


Iterate & Improve (20 min)
Why does it fail on someone else’s wrist? Collect more diverse samples. Compare accuracy before/after.
0:40–1:00
Key insight: data quality matters


MakeCode Response (30 min)
Export model to MakeCode. Program: walking → scroll message on LED; still → show symbol; other → show ‘?’.
1:00–1:30
Templates provided


Design Challenge (20 min)
Groups sketch their own gesture-based device concept for tomorrow: what 3–4 gestures? What human need does it serve?
1:30–1:50
Design brief template


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Discussion: How is this similar to scikit-learn? How is it different? Preview tomorrow’s build.
1:50–2:00








BBC micro:bit V2 (1 per 2–3 students) + USB cables + Chrome browser
createai.microbit.org + makecode.microbit.org
CreateAI getting started guide (microbit.org/createai)
Design brief template (1-pager)












Design and train a multi-class gesture model for a purposeful application
Write a program that takes meaningful action on detected gestures
Debug and iterate on model quality (test on different people, orientations)
Articulate the human role in AI: labeling, choosing classes, and testing













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Design Review (10 min)
Groups share their sketch from Day 4. Quick peer feedback: is the human need clear?
0:00–0:10



Build & Train (60 min)
Groups collect data, train, test, iterate. Prompt: ‘Does it still work on someone else’s arm? In a different room?’ Aim for 2–3 iteration cycles.
0:10–1:10
Iterate deliberately


Code the Response (35 min)
Write the MakeCode or Python program. Micro:bit responds with LED, sound, or serial output. Push toward a complete, working demo.
1:10–1:45



Demo Gallery (15 min)
Informal 2-min demo per group. Peers act as testers. Celebrate creative use of gestures.
1:45–2:00
Fun, low-stakes







micro:bits from Day 4
createai.microbit.org + MakeCode
Optional: buzzer accessories for audio feedback


Instructor Notes: Module 2 capstone. Accuracy matters less than purposeful design — celebrate a 70% accurate exercise counter more than a 95% accurate wave detector with no clear use. Great transition line: ‘Next week we’ll teach computers to see, not just move.’












Explain how a CNN classifies images at a conceptual level (pixels → features → predictions)
Run pre-trained classification, pose detection, and object detection models in the browser
Build a working CV application combining a pre-trained model with custom Python logic
Identify at least one ethical concern in computer vision technology













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Hook (10 min)
Show 10 images pixelated at varying levels. Students classify. ‘Computers start from pixels just like this bottom row.’
0:00–0:10



CV Concepts (20 min)
Instructor overview: pixels → CNN layers → predictions. Demo MobileNet on webcam in browser — confidence scores live. Show 3 task types: classification, pose detection, object detection.
0:10–0:30
TF.js demos, no GPU


Exploration Stations (25 min)
Sequential or parallel: (1) Image classification with MobileNet, (2) Pose detection with MoveNet, (3) Object detection with COCO-SSD. Students screenshot one result from each.
0:30–0:55
Pre-built browser demos


Ethics Discussion (15 min)
Case study: CV in surveillance, facial recognition bias. Small groups: ‘Should CV be used to monitor students in school?’ 2-min debrief.
0:55–1:10
Socratic format


Mini-Project Build (40 min)
Groups choose a project track: (A) Pose Counter — count reps when keypoint crosses threshold. (B) Object Zone Game — detect object entering a screen zone. (C) Virtual Background — segmentation + canvas overlay. (D) Expression Mirror — MediaPipe Face Mesh reactions.
1:10–1:50
Template notebooks per track


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Quick share: one unexpected CV challenge you hit. Preview: tomorrow we add language to vision.
1:50–2:00








TensorFlow.js browser demos (tensorflow.org/js/demos)
MediaPipe (mediapipe.dev) for pose/face
Project template notebooks (one per track)
Colab with webcam access (JS bridge) or pre-recorded video fallback












Describe what a VLM does and how it differs from a classifier
Explain tokenization with a hands-on example
Describe word embeddings as ‘coordinates for meaning’ in vector space
Explain the transformer architecture at a high level and connect it to VLMs













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




VLM Demo & Discussion (20 min)
Instructor shows a VLM describing images — impressive case, then a failure case. ‘How is this different from MobileNet?’ Discussion: it produces language, not just a label. Show the two-component model: visual encoder + language model.
0:00–0:20
Claude API, GPT-4V, or HuggingFace Spaces (free)


VLM Exploration (25 min)
Students query a VLM with their CV project screenshots + Mongolia/Naadam photos. Task: find one impressive result and one failure. Share findings.
0:20–0:45
No API key needed via HuggingFace


Bridge: From Vision to Language (5 min)
Quick pivot: ‘VLMs describe images. What if AI could generate language from scratch — or generate images?’ Introduce Module 4.
0:45–0:50



Tokenization Activity (20 min)
Students use platform.openai.com/tokenizer on Mongolian words, code, emoji, and their own name. Questions: how many tokens? Why does it matter for cost and context?
0:50–1:10
Physical token cards as backup


Embeddings Demo (20 min)
Word2vec/semantic similarity: ‘King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen.’ Students try analogies in a Colab notebook. Instructor explains: these are the coordinates transformers learn from data.
1:10–1:30
Colab + gensim


Transformer Overview (20 min)
Visual walkthrough of attention using ‘The bank by the river’ example. No math — diagrams only. Connect: ‘The LLM is doing this for every token, millions of times, trained on the same kind of data you used in Week 1.’
1:30–1:50
Jay Alammar visuals


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Exit ticket: draw the path from ‘user types a message’ → ‘LLM responds.’ Preview tomorrow’s chatbot build.
1:50–2:00








VLM access: HuggingFace Spaces (free LLaVA demo), Claude API, or GPT-4V
platform.openai.com/tokenizer (free, no login)
Colab notebook: gensim word embeddings
Jay Alammar ‘Illustrated Transformer’ diagrams (jalammar.github.io)












Apply 4+ prompt engineering techniques: persona, few-shot, chain-of-thought, constraints
Use Code.org’s AI Chat Lab to design and build a custom chatbot with a system prompt
Evaluate a chatbot’s responses critically and iterate on the design
Explain how system prompts shape model behavior













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Prompt Warm-Up (15 min)
Instructor shows the same question with 3 different prompts → very different outputs. Students guess which prompt produced which. Introduce: persona, few-shot, CoT, constraints.
0:00–0:15



Prompt Engineering Practice (25 min)
Students experiment with a live LLM: explain photosynthesis to a 5-year-old vs. a PhD; add a constraint (‘no jargon’); try chain-of-thought for a logic problem.
0:15–0:40
Claude.ai or Code.org AI Chat Lab


Chatbot Design Intro (10 min)
Introduce Code.org’s Exploring Generative AI curriculum and AI Chat Lab. Walk through system prompt anatomy. Show a pre-built ‘Cat Expert’ chatbot.
0:40–0:50
code.org — free, grades 8–12


Build Your Chatbot (60 min)
Groups of 2 design and build a chatbot on a topic they care about. Requirements: clear persona, 3 topic focus areas, 1 ‘do not discuss’ boundary, tested with 5+ different questions.
0:50–1:50



Wrap-Up (10 min)
Share chatbot name + one surprising thing it did. Preview: tomorrow you’ll add knowledge and show it off — plus explore image generation.
1:50–2:00








Code.org AI Chat Lab (code.org/en-US/curriculum/exploring-generative-artificial-intelligence)
Prompt engineering reference card (1-pager: 4 techniques with examples)
Example system prompts to study and remix
Claude.ai or similar LLM for free-form practice












Deliver a polished chatbot demo including a system prompt, knowledge grounding, and graceful failure
Explain how diffusion models generate images at a conceptual level
Craft effective image generation prompts using a structured scaffold
Engage with IP and ethics questions around AI-generated art













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




RAG & Knowledge Grounding (15 min)
Quick demo: same chatbot with and without a knowledge document. What changed? Why? Conceptual explanation of retrieval-augmented generation — no code required.
0:00–0:15



Polish Your Chatbot (25 min)
Groups add a knowledge document (or expanded system prompt with facts) and evaluate using a peer rubric: helpfulness, accuracy, personality, graceful failure.
0:15–0:40
Printed rubric


Chatbot Showcase (20 min)
Each group demos in 2 minutes: what it does, a successful exchange, something it won’t do. Peer votes: Most Creative, Most Useful, Best Personality.
0:40–1:00
Celebration atmosphere


Diffusion Model Concepts (15 min)
How AI generates images: noise → denoise → image, latent space, text encoder. Visual only — no math. ‘This is the visual twin of what the LLM does with tokens.’
1:00–1:15
Show denoising animation


Image Generation Workshop (30 min)
Students generate images on Replicate (or Adobe Firefly / Canva AI). Prompt scaffold: [subject] + [style] + [lighting] + [mood]. Theme: inspired by Mongolia or Naadam. Iterate 3 times.
1:15–1:45
Replicate.com or free alternatives


Ethics: Art & AI (10 min)
Quick discussion: Who owns AI-generated art? Should models be trained on artists’ work without consent? 2-min think-pair-share.
1:45–1:55



Preview Final Day (5 min)
Explain tomorrow’s capstone structure. Remind groups to choose their showcase project.
1:55–2:00








Code.org AI Chat Lab
Peer chatbot evaluation rubric (4 dimensions, 1 page)
Replicate.com (hosted image models) or Adobe Firefly / Canva AI (free)
Diffusion explainer clip (Computerphile or 3Blue1Brown, ~5 min)
Prompt scaffold template (printable)












Synthesize learning across all four modules in a final project presentation
Demonstrate genuine understanding of at least one AI concept to peers
Articulate one societal implication of AI in a domain they care about
Leave feeling capable, curious, and inspired to keep building













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Setup & Last-Mile Polish (20 min)
Groups do final prep on their capstone project. Instructor confirms running order.
0:00–0:20



Final Project Presentations (60 min)
Each group: 3–4 min presentation + 1 min Q&A. Project options: (A) Data Story — extended Module 1 investigation; (B) Physical AI Device — polished micro:bit project with docs; (C) CV Application — finished Module 3 project with demo; (D) Chatbot — polished bot with knowledge base; (E) Wild Card — cross-module project combining 2+ AI types.
0:20–1:20
All groups present


Letter to Myself (15 min)
Each student writes: one AI application they want to build in the next year, and one thing they commit to thinking critically about.
1:20–1:35
Private or share optionally


Panel Reflection (10 min)
Student-led open Q&A: What surprised you most? What do you wish AI could do that it can’t? What worries you?
1:35–1:45



Celebration & Certificates (15 min)
Award recognition in all categories. Share ‘What’s Next’ resource card. Group photo.
1:45–2:00
Certificates printed in advance







Cross-module presentation rubric (technical accuracy, creativity, communication, ethics awareness)
Certificate template
“What to Explore Next” card: fast.ai · Kaggle Learn · CS50 AI · 3Blue1Brown · deeplearning.ai short courses
Optional: highlight reel compiled from Days 1–9

Week 1, Day 1

Week 1, Days 2–3

Week 1, Days 4–5

Week 2, Day 6
Module 3: Computer Vision
CNNs, TF.js, MediaPipe, CV mini-projects

VLMs, tokens, embeddings, transformer overview


Week 2, Days 8–9

Prompt engineering, chatbots, diffusion, image gen


Week 2, Day 10
Capstone Showcase
Final project presentations, reflection, celebration

text:
Open a Colab notebook, run cells, and load a CSV with pandas
Use .head(), .describe(), .value_counts(), and filtering to investigate a real question
Produce at least one meaningful matplotlib visualization that supports a finding
Explain why data quality and selection matter for AI — using today’s dataset as evidence













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




The Hook: A Mystery (10 min)
Instructor opens with a single striking fact: in January, Ulaanbaatar regularly records PM2.5 levels 30–40× the WHO safe limit — worse than Beijing, worse than Delhi. Show a before/after satellite photo of the city in summer vs. winter smog. ‘Your job today: figure out why, using data.’ Introduce the detective framing: the dataset is your crime scene.
0:00–0:10
Show satellite comparison image


Setup & Tools (15 min)
Walk through Google Colab. Every student runs: print('Investigation begins!'). Quick pandas demo: DataFrames, .head(), .info(), .describe() — framed as ‘reading the crime scene.’
0:10–0:25
Pair struggling students


Investigation Sprint (40 min)
Groups of 3 dig into the Ulaanbaatar AQI dataset with guided question cards (not open-ended — each card is a specific clue to chase). E.g., ‘Which month has the worst PM2.5? Which has the best?’, ‘Is there a pattern by day of week?’, ‘Does temperature predict pollution level?’ Groups record findings in a shared doc.
0:25–1:05
Question cards pre-prepared


Visualize Your Evidence (25 min)
Each group turns their strongest finding into one chart using matplotlib templates. Frame: ‘A detective doesn’t just tell the jury — they show the evidence.’
1:05–1:30
Templates in notebook


AI Training Data Lens (20 min)
Pivot: ‘Now imagine you’re an AI company and this is your training data for a pollution prediction model. Would it be good enough? What’s missing? What would your model get wrong?’ Small group discussion, then share out.
1:30–1:50
Connects directly to Week 2


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Each group posts their one-sentence finding to a class board. Preview: tomorrow you get an AI assistant to help with a harder dataset — and you’ll decide if it’s trustworthy.
1:50–2:00








Google Colab (browser, no install)
Ulaanbaatar historical AQI dataset (PM2.5, PM10, temperature, season — CSV, pre-cleaned from aqicn.org or IQAir historical download)
Starter notebook with scaffolded cells + matplotlib templates
Investigation question cards (printed, one set per group — 6–8 clues to chase)
Shared class findings doc (Google Doc or Jamboard)
Satellite comparison images: Ulaanbaatar summer vs. winter smog (freely available)

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Use a Claude-powered data assistant to query a dataset in plain English
Critically evaluate AI-generated interpretations against actual data
Train a simple linear regression model and interpret its predictions
Articulate the direct conceptual link between regression modeling and the micro:bit ML training in Module 2













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




The Hook: Dzud (10 min)
Instructor introduces the dzud — Mongolia’s winter livestock disaster. Show the stat: in 2010, 20 million animals died in a single winter. In 2024, 7.4 million in just four months. ‘Herders knew a bad summer meant a bad winter. Can a machine learn that relationship from data?’ Introduce today’s dataset: aimag-level livestock mortality, summer drought index, and winter temperature, 1990–2013.
0:00–0:10
Map of Mongolia’s 21 aimags on screen


Introduce the AI Co-Pilot (15 min)
Instructor demo: a Claude-powered chat interface loaded with the dzud dataset context. Ask it: ‘Which aimag suffered the most in 2010?’ Then ask something it gets wrong or oversimplifies. ‘Your job: use this tool as a starting point, then verify everything in code.’
0:10–0:25
Claude.ai Project or Colab widget


Investigation: Human + AI (35 min)
Groups explore the dataset with the AI co-pilot. Workflow: ask the assistant a question → verify in pandas → document where it’s right, wrong, or uncertain. Each group must catch the AI making at least 2 errors or oversimplifications before they present.
0:25–1:00
Encourage skepticism explicitly


Linear Regression: Predict the Disaster (25 min)
Instructor-led walkthrough: train a linear regression model to predict livestock mortality from summer drought index and winter temperature. Students run it step-by-step. Key question: ‘This model sees the summer data — can it warn a herder before winter arrives?’
1:00–1:25
Heavily scaffolded notebook


The Bridge Moment (5 min)
Instructor draws the explicit connection: ‘Yesterday we looked for patterns in pollution data. Today we trained a model to predict disasters from patterns in climate data. On Thursday, you’ll do the exact same thing — but the data will be your own body movement, and the micro:bit will be the device that learns.’ Plant the seed.
1:25–1:30
Key conceptual moment — don’t rush


Data Story Presentations (20 min)
Each group: 3 min presentation — the question, the evidence, one thing the AI got wrong, one limitation of the dataset.
1:30–1:50
Rubric: story, evidence, AI critique, limitation


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Class vote: whose finding was most surprising? ‘What does it feel like to catch an AI being wrong?’ Preview: micro:bits arrive tomorrow.
1:50–2:00








Claude-powered data assistant (Claude.ai Project with dataset summary as context, or a lightweight Colab widget using the Anthropic API)
Dzud dataset: aimag-level livestock mortality + summer drought index + winter temperature, 1990–2013 (sourced from Mongolian NSO / Rao et al. 2015 supplementary data)
Colab notebook with linear regression walkthrough + visualization templates
Map of Mongolia’s 21 aimags (printed or on-screen)
Presentation rubric (story, evidence, AI critique, limitation — 1 page)
Optional: class leaderboard for ‘best AI catch of the day’

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Connect a micro:bit to CreateAI and view live accelerometer data in real time
Train a gesture model with at least 3 classes and explain what ‘confidence threshold’ means
Understand that ML models learn from examples, not explicit rules
Write a MakeCode program that responds meaningfully to detected gestures













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Hardware Orientation (15 min)
Distribute micro:bits (groups of 2–3). Connect via USB, open createai.microbit.org. Instructor demo: accelerometer graph while shaking vs. still.
0:00–0:15
V2 required for on-device ML


First Model: Walk vs. Still (25 min)
Guided: everyone trains the same model — ‘walking’ vs. ‘still’ vs. ‘other’. Collect 20 samples per class. Train and test on each other.
0:15–0:40
Use CreateAI guided tour


Iterate & Improve (20 min)
Why does it fail on someone else’s wrist? Collect more diverse samples. Compare accuracy before/after.
0:40–1:00
Key insight: data quality matters


MakeCode Response (30 min)
Export model to MakeCode. Program: walking → scroll message on LED; still → show symbol; other → show ‘?’.
1:00–1:30
Templates provided


Design Challenge (20 min)
Groups sketch their own gesture-based device concept for tomorrow: what 3–4 gestures? What human need does it serve?
1:30–1:50
Design brief template


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Discussion: How is this similar to scikit-learn? How is it different? Preview tomorrow’s build.
1:50–2:00








BBC micro:bit V2 (1 per 2–3 students) + USB cables + Chrome browser
createai.microbit.org + makecode.microbit.org
CreateAI getting started guide (microbit.org/createai)
Design brief template (1-pager)

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Design and train a multi-class gesture model for a purposeful application
Write a program that takes meaningful action on detected gestures
Debug and iterate on model quality (test on different people, orientations)
Articulate the human role in AI: labeling, choosing classes, and testing













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Design Review (10 min)
Groups share their sketch from Day 4. Quick peer feedback: is the human need clear?
0:00–0:10



Build & Train (60 min)
Groups collect data, train, test, iterate. Prompt: ‘Does it still work on someone else’s arm? In a different room?’ Aim for 2–3 iteration cycles.
0:10–1:10
Iterate deliberately


Code the Response (35 min)
Write the MakeCode or Python program. Micro:bit responds with LED, sound, or serial output. Push toward a complete, working demo.
1:10–1:45



Demo Gallery (15 min)
Informal 2-min demo per group. Peers act as testers. Celebrate creative use of gestures.
1:45–2:00
Fun, low-stakes







micro:bits from Day 4
createai.microbit.org + MakeCode
Optional: buzzer accessories for audio feedback


Instructor Notes: Module 2 capstone. Accuracy matters less than purposeful design — celebrate a 70% accurate exercise counter more than a 95% accurate wave detector with no clear use. Great transition line: ‘Next week we’ll teach computers to see, not just move.’

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Explain how a CNN classifies images at a conceptual level (pixels → features → predictions)
Run pre-trained classification, pose detection, and object detection models in the browser
Build a working CV application combining a pre-trained model with custom Python logic
Identify at least one ethical concern in computer vision technology













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Hook (10 min)
Show 10 images pixelated at varying levels. Students classify. ‘Computers start from pixels just like this bottom row.’
0:00–0:10



CV Concepts (20 min)
Instructor overview: pixels → CNN layers → predictions. Demo MobileNet on webcam in browser — confidence scores live. Show 3 task types: classification, pose detection, object detection.
0:10–0:30
TF.js demos, no GPU


Exploration Stations (25 min)
Sequential or parallel: (1) Image classification with MobileNet, (2) Pose detection with MoveNet, (3) Object detection with COCO-SSD. Students screenshot one result from each.
0:30–0:55
Pre-built browser demos


Ethics Discussion (15 min)
Case study: CV in surveillance, facial recognition bias. Small groups: ‘Should CV be used to monitor students in school?’ 2-min debrief.
0:55–1:10
Socratic format


Mini-Project Build (40 min)
Groups choose a project track: (A) Pose Counter — count reps when keypoint crosses threshold. (B) Object Zone Game — detect object entering a screen zone. (C) Virtual Background — segmentation + canvas overlay. (D) Expression Mirror — MediaPipe Face Mesh reactions.
1:10–1:50
Template notebooks per track


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Quick share: one unexpected CV challenge you hit. Preview: tomorrow we add language to vision.
1:50–2:00








TensorFlow.js browser demos (tensorflow.org/js/demos)
MediaPipe (mediapipe.dev) for pose/face
Project template notebooks (one per track)
Colab with webcam access (JS bridge) or pre-recorded video fallback

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Describe what a VLM does and how it differs from a classifier
Explain tokenization with a hands-on example
Describe word embeddings as ‘coordinates for meaning’ in vector space
Explain the transformer architecture at a high level and connect it to VLMs













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




VLM Demo & Discussion (20 min)
Instructor shows a VLM describing images — impressive case, then a failure case. ‘How is this different from MobileNet?’ Discussion: it produces language, not just a label. Show the two-component model: visual encoder + language model.
0:00–0:20
Claude API, GPT-4V, or HuggingFace Spaces (free)


VLM Exploration (25 min)
Students query a VLM with their CV project screenshots + Mongolia/Naadam photos. Task: find one impressive result and one failure. Share findings.
0:20–0:45
No API key needed via HuggingFace


Bridge: From Vision to Language (5 min)
Quick pivot: ‘VLMs describe images. What if AI could generate language from scratch — or generate images?’ Introduce Module 4.
0:45–0:50



Tokenization Activity (20 min)
Students use platform.openai.com/tokenizer on Mongolian words, code, emoji, and their own name. Questions: how many tokens? Why does it matter for cost and context?
0:50–1:10
Physical token cards as backup


Embeddings Demo (20 min)
Word2vec/semantic similarity: ‘King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen.’ Students try analogies in a Colab notebook. Instructor explains: these are the coordinates transformers learn from data.
1:10–1:30
Colab + gensim


Transformer Overview (20 min)
Visual walkthrough of attention using ‘The bank by the river’ example. No math — diagrams only. Connect: ‘The LLM is doing this for every token, millions of times, trained on the same kind of data you used in Week 1.’
1:30–1:50
Jay Alammar visuals


Wrap-Up (10 min)
Exit ticket: draw the path from ‘user types a message’ → ‘LLM responds.’ Preview tomorrow’s chatbot build.
1:50–2:00








VLM access: HuggingFace Spaces (free LLaVA demo), Claude API, or GPT-4V
platform.openai.com/tokenizer (free, no login)
Colab notebook: gensim word embeddings
Jay Alammar ‘Illustrated Transformer’ diagrams (jalammar.github.io)

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Apply 4+ prompt engineering techniques: persona, few-shot, chain-of-thought, constraints
Use Code.org’s AI Chat Lab to design and build a custom chatbot with a system prompt
Evaluate a chatbot’s responses critically and iterate on the design
Explain how system prompts shape model behavior













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Prompt Warm-Up (15 min)
Instructor shows the same question with 3 different prompts → very different outputs. Students guess which prompt produced which. Introduce: persona, few-shot, CoT, constraints.
0:00–0:15



Prompt Engineering Practice (25 min)
Students experiment with a live LLM: explain photosynthesis to a 5-year-old vs. a PhD; add a constraint (‘no jargon’); try chain-of-thought for a logic problem.
0:15–0:40
Claude.ai or Code.org AI Chat Lab


Chatbot Design Intro (10 min)
Introduce Code.org’s Exploring Generative AI curriculum and AI Chat Lab. Walk through system prompt anatomy. Show a pre-built ‘Cat Expert’ chatbot.
0:40–0:50
code.org — free, grades 8–12


Build Your Chatbot (60 min)
Groups of 2 design and build a chatbot on a topic they care about. Requirements: clear persona, 3 topic focus areas, 1 ‘do not discuss’ boundary, tested with 5+ different questions.
0:50–1:50



Wrap-Up (10 min)
Share chatbot name + one surprising thing it did. Preview: tomorrow you’ll add knowledge and show it off — plus explore image generation.
1:50–2:00








Code.org AI Chat Lab (code.org/en-US/curriculum/exploring-generative-artificial-intelligence)
Prompt engineering reference card (1-pager: 4 techniques with examples)
Example system prompts to study and remix
Claude.ai or similar LLM for free-form practice

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Deliver a polished chatbot demo including a system prompt, knowledge grounding, and graceful failure
Explain how diffusion models generate images at a conceptual level
Craft effective image generation prompts using a structured scaffold
Engage with IP and ethics questions around AI-generated art













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




RAG & Knowledge Grounding (15 min)
Quick demo: same chatbot with and without a knowledge document. What changed? Why? Conceptual explanation of retrieval-augmented generation — no code required.
0:00–0:15



Polish Your Chatbot (25 min)
Groups add a knowledge document (or expanded system prompt with facts) and evaluate using a peer rubric: helpfulness, accuracy, personality, graceful failure.
0:15–0:40
Printed rubric


Chatbot Showcase (20 min)
Each group demos in 2 minutes: what it does, a successful exchange, something it won’t do. Peer votes: Most Creative, Most Useful, Best Personality.
0:40–1:00
Celebration atmosphere


Diffusion Model Concepts (15 min)
How AI generates images: noise → denoise → image, latent space, text encoder. Visual only — no math. ‘This is the visual twin of what the LLM does with tokens.’
1:00–1:15
Show denoising animation


Image Generation Workshop (30 min)
Students generate images on Replicate (or Adobe Firefly / Canva AI). Prompt scaffold: [subject] + [style] + [lighting] + [mood]. Theme: inspired by Mongolia or Naadam. Iterate 3 times.
1:15–1:45
Replicate.com or free alternatives


Ethics: Art & AI (10 min)
Quick discussion: Who owns AI-generated art? Should models be trained on artists’ work without consent? 2-min think-pair-share.
1:45–1:55



Preview Final Day (5 min)
Explain tomorrow’s capstone structure. Remind groups to choose their showcase project.
1:55–2:00








Code.org AI Chat Lab
Peer chatbot evaluation rubric (4 dimensions, 1 page)
Replicate.com (hosted image models) or Adobe Firefly / Canva AI (free)
Diffusion explainer clip (Computerphile or 3Blue1Brown, ~5 min)
Prompt scaffold template (printable)

title: Summer of Code 2026
text:
Synthesize learning across all four modules in a final project presentation
Demonstrate genuine understanding of at least one AI concept to peers
Articulate one societal implication of AI in a domain they care about
Leave feeling capable, curious, and inspired to keep building













Segment
Details
Time
Notes




Setup & Last-Mile Polish (20 min)
Groups do final prep on their capstone project. Instructor confirms running order.
0:00–0:20



Final Project Presentations (60 min)
Each group: 3–4 min presentation + 1 min Q&A. Project options: (A) Data Story — extended Module 1 investigation; (B) Physical AI Device — polished micro:bit project with docs; (C) CV Application — finished Module 3 project with demo; (D) Chatbot — polished bot with knowledge base; (E) Wild Card — cross-module project combining 2+ AI types.
0:20–1:20
All groups present


Letter to Myself (15 min)
Each student writes: one AI application they want to build in the next year, and one thing they commit to thinking critically about.
1:20–1:35
Private or share optionally


Panel Reflection (10 min)
Student-led open Q&A: What surprised you most? What do you wish AI could do that it can’t? What worries you?
1:35–1:45



Celebration & Certificates (15 min)
Award recognition in all categories. Share ‘What’s Next’ resource card. Group photo.
1:45–2:00
Certificates printed in advance







Cross-module presentation rubric (technical accuracy, creativity, communication, ethics awareness)
Certificate template
“What to Explore Next” card: fast.ai · Kaggle Learn · CS50 AI · 3Blue1Brown · deeplearning.ai short courses
Optional: highlight reel compiled from Days 1–9
